So, the process, what it looks like, it's similar to Aerofs, in the sense that it starts with an assignment brief, goes through to the research stage.
The assignment brief is where we give human input, so this is where we can, like, tailor the angle of a specific article.
The research stage is when the LLM goes.
And collects like available data about the specific topic so that it can actually write the article in an accurate way.
Then that information is fed into the outline.
We also tweak the outline.
This is where we control the length of specific sections like the H2s, H3s.
Once we like finalize the outline, we move to article draft.
And after that comes the fact-checking process.
The fact-checker in Atlas is much more advanced than what we were using before.

I have used a fact-checker on other clients and it will literally, if it finds a claim that is unsubstantiated, it's going to insert a paragraph that says that, explaining that there is no evidence to back up a specific claim.
So if there's a piece of research that it mentions or something.
So...
So...
So...
It really goes pretty deep.
And it rewrites sections as well.
So if there's a stat that is incorrect, it's going to go and find the actual number, go back and rewrite that whole sentence or paragraph, and then send it back.
Obviously, we have the human review at the end.
So that's also something that we pay attention to during the editing part of the process.
But as you can see, quite similar to, I know I did an Aeroops walkthrough with you guys like a few months ago.
So it's a similar process, but it's more advanced.
And also just editing the final article is a lot easier in Atlas because it's just a lot faster.
So that's great.
